section.title: Peter's Confession
section.sectionRationale: This is the hinge of Mark's Gospel. At Caesarea Philippi Peter confesses Jesus as the Christ, and immediately Jesus begins to teach that the Christ must suffer, be killed, and rise. Peter rebukes Him and is rebuked as Satan. Jesus then defines discipleship itself as cross-bearing: to follow Him is to deny self, lose one's life to save it, and never be ashamed of Him.
section.thesis: Jesus is indeed the Christ, but a Christ who must suffer and die and rise, and to follow this Messiah is to take up the cross, so that only the one who loses his life for Jesus and the gospel will truly save it.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the confession of Peter; the first prediction of the Passion and Peter's rebuke; the call to take up the cross; and the promise that the kingdom will come with power.

verses.text: Then Jesus and His disciples went on to the villages around Caesarea Philippi. On the way, He questioned His disciples: “Who do people say I am?”
They replied, “Some say John the Baptist; others say Elijah; and still others, one of the prophets.”
“But what about you?” Jesus asked. “Who do you say I am?” Peter answered, “You are the {{christos|Christ}}.”
And Jesus warned them not to tell anyone about Him.

units.label: The first prediction of the Passion
units.note: the Son of Man must suffer

verses.text: Then He began to teach them that the Son of Man must suffer many things and be rejected by the elders, chief priests, and scribes, and that He must be killed and after three days rise again.
He spoke this message quite frankly, and Peter took Him aside and began to rebuke Him.
But Jesus, turning and looking at His disciples, rebuked Peter and said, “Get behind Me, Satan! For you do not have in mind the things of God, but the things of men.”

units.label: Take up your cross
units.note: whoever loses his life for My sake will save it

verses.text: Then Jesus called the crowd to Him along with His disciples, and He told them, “If anyone wants to come after Me, he must {{aparneomai|deny}} himself and take up his {{stauros|cross}} and follow Me.
For whoever wants to save his life will lose it, but whoever loses his life for My sake and for the gospel will save it.
What does it profit a man to gain the whole world, yet forfeit his {{psyche|soul}}?
Or what can a man give in exchange for his soul?
If anyone is ashamed of Me and My words in this adulterous and sinful generation, the Son of Man will also be ashamed of him when He comes in His Father’s glory with the holy angels.”

units.label: The kingdom coming with power
units.note: some will not taste death before they see it

verses.text: Then Jesus said to them, “Truly I tell you, there are some standing here who will not taste death before they see the kingdom of God arrive with power.”

christos.gloss: Anointed One, Messiah, the promised King of David's line. Peter's confession is right and pivotal, but incomplete, for he does not yet grasp that this Christ must reign through suffering and death. Jesus accepts the title and at once redefines it by the cross.

aparneomai.gloss: To deny, disown, renounce. To deny oneself is to renounce self-rule and self-interest as the centre of life, handing over the throne of one's heart to Jesus. The same verb describes Peter's later denial of Jesus, a sobering counterpoint to this call.

stauros.gloss: A cross, the Roman instrument of execution carried by the condemned to the place of death. To take up one's cross is not merely to bear hardship but to walk the road of death to self, following the crucified Messiah wherever it leads.

psyche.gloss: Life, soul, the whole self. Jesus plays on its double sense: grasping to save one's earthly life is to forfeit one's true and eternal self, while losing one's life for Him and the gospel is to save it. Nothing gained in the world can outweigh the soul.

basic.context: On the road near Caesarea Philippi, Jesus asks His disciples who people say He is, and then who they say He is. Peter answers rightly: You are the Christ. But when Jesus begins to teach that the Christ must suffer, be rejected, be killed, and rise, Peter takes Him aside to rebuke Him, and Jesus turns and rebukes Peter as Satan, for thinking like men, not God. Then Jesus tells the crowd and disciples what following Him means: deny yourself, take up your cross, and follow Him. Whoever tries to save his life will lose it; whoever loses it for Jesus and the gospel will save it. To be ashamed of Him now is to be disowned when He comes in glory. And He promises that some standing there will see the kingdom come with power.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the turning point of Mark. Up to now the question has been who Jesus is; from here the question is what kind of Christ He is, and what kind of disciples His followers must be. Peter's right confession and immediate error show that even true faith can misunderstand the way of the cross. Jesus binds His identity and His disciples' path together: the suffering Messiah calls a cross-bearing people.
basic.originalLanguages: Peter confesses Jesus as the christos, the Messiah. Jesus calls disciples to aparneomai, deny, themselves and take up the stauros, the cross. To cling to one's psyche, one's life or soul, is to lose it; to lose it for Him is to save it.
basic.typology: The suffering Son of Man fulfils the pattern of the suffering servant of Isaiah 53 and the rejected stone of the Psalms. Taking up the cross to follow the crucified King anticipates the whole shape of Christian discipleship.
basic.christ: Jesus is the Christ, but a crucified and risen Christ. He is the Son of Man who must suffer, be killed, and rise, and who will return in the Father's glory. His identity and His mission cannot be separated.
basic.god: God's way is not the way of human power and self-preservation but of the cross. To have in mind the things of God is to embrace the suffering path Jesus walks and calls His people to walk.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 53 foretells the suffering servant. Psalm 118 speaks of the rejected stone. Philippians 2 traces Christ's humbling and exaltation.
basic.oneStory: The long expectation of the Messiah reaches its clarifying moment. Jesus is the Christ, but He will reign by dying and rising, not by conquering as Israel hoped. And those who follow Him must take up their own crosses. The whole Gospel now turns toward Jerusalem and the cross where the Christ will save His people by losing His life.

context.literary: Caesarea Philippi is the structural centre of Mark. The first half asks, who is this? and reaches its answer here in Peter's confession; the second half unfolds what it means that the Christ must suffer, as Jesus predicts His death three times on the road to Jerusalem, 8:31, 9:31, and 10:33. The blind man healed in two stages just before mirrors Peter's partial sight.
context.historical: Caesarea Philippi lay in the far north, a Gentile city with pagan shrines, a striking place for the confession of Israel's Messiah. First century Jewish hope largely expected a triumphant, conquering Messiah who would defeat Rome, which is why a suffering, dying Christ was so scandalous, even to a disciple like Peter.
context.canonical: Peter's confession, expanded in Matthew 16:16, stands as the church's foundational creed. Jesus' redefinition of Messiah by suffering draws on Isaiah 52:13 to 53:12 and the rejected stone of Psalm 118:22. The call to take up the cross and the warning about being ashamed of Him echo through the New Testament, Romans 1:16 and 2 Timothy 2:12.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To mark the Gospel's turning point by establishing that Jesus is the Christ and immediately redefining that title by the necessity of His suffering, death, and resurrection, and to bind the disciples' path to His own: to follow the crucified Messiah is to take up the cross.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The confession and the Passion prediction describe who Jesus is and what He came to do. The call to deny self, take up the cross, and lose one's life for Him is prescriptive for every disciple in every age, though the particular form of the cross varies. The promise of verse 1 concerns a specific fulfilment, not an open-ended prediction.

debates.question: Why does Jesus call Peter Satan so sharply after such a good confession?

views.label: Peter voices the tempter's agenda
views.case: By urging a crown without a cross, Peter echoes the wilderness temptation to seize glory without suffering; Jesus names the source of that thinking, for it would derail the very mission He came to accomplish.

views.label: A rebuke of merely human thinking
views.case: Peter has in mind the things of men, a Messiah on human terms; Jesus corrects not Peter's love but his category, calling him and all disciples to think God's thoughts about the cross.

debates.bearing: Both readings hold that resisting the cross, however well-meant, opposes God's saving plan; the way of the Christ and of His followers runs through suffering to glory.

debates.question: What did Jesus mean that some standing there would see the kingdom come with power?

views.label: The transfiguration, resurrection, and Pentecost
views.case: Within the lifetime of the hearers, the kingdom broke in with power at the transfiguration that immediately follows, and supremely in Jesus' resurrection and the coming of the Spirit, inaugurating the reign of God.

views.label: The whole complex of Christ's saving triumph
views.case: The saying points to the decisive arrival of God's reign in the death, resurrection, ascension, and Spirit-empowered spread of the gospel, all seen by that generation, rather than to the final return.

debates.bearing: Both readings locate the fulfilment within the hearers' lifetime in the events that established Christ's reign, not in a failed prediction of an immediate end.

study.originalLanguages: Peter rightly names Jesus the christos, the Messiah, the anointed King, but at once shows he does not yet grasp what kind of King. Jesus redefines the title by the cross and then defines discipleship by it: to come after Him one must aparneomai, deny, oneself, renouncing self-rule, and take up the stauros, the cross, the condemned man's instrument of death. Then He turns on its head the instinct to self-preservation: whoever grasps to save his psyche, his life and soul, will lose it, but whoever loses it for Jesus and the gospel will save it. The vocabulary fuses Christ's path and the disciple's into one road that leads through death to life.

typology.fulfillment: Jesus fulfilling the suffering servant who is despised, rejected, and pierced for our transgressions, Isaiah 53:3-5.
The Christ rejected by the builders yet made the cornerstone, Psalm 118:22 and Mark 12:10.
The pattern of Christ's own humbling and exaltation, extended to all who follow Him, Philippians 2:8-11 and John 12:24-25.

study.christ: Here the veil lifts and then is redrawn. Jesus is the Christ, Peter's confession is true, but Mark will not let us keep a Messiah of glory without a cross. Jesus is the Son of Man who must suffer, be rejected, be killed, and after three days rise; His identity and His Passion are inseparable. When Peter tries to pull them apart, Jesus rebukes him as Satan, for a crownless cross is the tempter's lie. And the same Christ who will die and rise will return in the Father's glory with the holy angels, so that being ashamed of Him now determines one's standing then. He is the crucified, risen, and returning King.
study.god: The passage overturns human ideas of how God saves and reigns. God's way is not the way of power, comfort, and self-preservation but the way of the cross; to have in mind the things of God is to accept that the Messiah must suffer and that His people must take up their crosses. God values the soul above the whole world and calls each person to lose their life in order to find it. The God who plans salvation through the shame of the cross also promises the glory of the kingdom coming with power, so that the road of suffering ends in resurrection and reign.

crossReferences.note: The suffering servant despised and rejected, pierced for our transgressions, the pattern of the suffering Son of Man.
The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone, the rejection Jesus foretells and later applies to Himself.
Christ humbled to death on a cross and then exalted, the losing-of-life-to-save-it pattern in full.
If we deny Him, He will deny us, echoing Jesus' warning about being ashamed of Him.

study.oneStory: Israel's hope for the Messiah reaches its decisive, clarifying moment: Jesus is the Christ. But in the same breath Jesus reveals that He will reign not by crushing Rome but by being crushed for sinners, killed and raised. The disciples, and we, must let go of a glory-only Messiah and follow a crucified one, taking up our own crosses. The whole Gospel now turns south toward Jerusalem and the cross, where the Christ will save His people by losing His life, and beyond it to the kingdom coming with power in His resurrection and the age to come.

quiz.q: How did Peter answer Jesus' question, who do you say I am?

options.text: You are the Christ, the promised Messiah.
options.note: Mark 8:29; Peter's true and pivotal confession.

options.text: You are a great prophet like Elijah or John.
options.note: That was what others said; Peter went further and confessed Him as the Christ.

options.text: You are the anointed King, though Peter did not yet grasp the suffering it involved.
options.note: Accurate and insightful, but the plain answer Peter gave is You are the Christ.

options.text: You are only a teacher sent from God.
options.note: Peter confessed far more: the Christ, not merely a teacher.

quiz.q: What did Jesus immediately begin to teach after Peter's confession?

options.text: That the Son of Man must suffer, be rejected, be killed, and after three days rise again.
options.note: Mark 8:31; Jesus redefines the Messiah by the cross and resurrection.

options.text: That the Messiah would soon overthrow the Romans.
options.note: The opposite of Jesus' teaching; He spoke of suffering and death, not political conquest.

options.text: That He would suffer and die for the sins of His people.
options.note: True to His mission, but here Mark records specifically the suffering, rejection, death, and rising He foretold.

options.text: That the disciples would rule with Him in glory at once.
options.note: Glory comes, but only through the cross; Jesus first taught His suffering, not immediate rule.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus rebuke Peter as Satan?

options.text: Because Peter, resisting the cross, had in mind the things of men, not the things of God.
options.note: Mark 8:33; opposing the necessary suffering echoed the tempter's agenda.

options.text: Because Peter had spoken a false confession.
options.note: Peter's confession was true; his error was resisting the way of the cross.

options.text: Because Peter tried to spare Jesus from suffering, thinking in human terms.
options.note: Correct in substance, but the sharpest point is that this was the things of men opposing God's plan.

options.text: Because Peter wanted to follow Jesus too closely.
options.note: The issue was not closeness but Peter's human-minded rejection of the cross.

quiz.q: What does Jesus say it means to follow Him?

options.text: To deny oneself, take up one's cross, and follow Him, losing one's life for Him and the gospel to truly save it.
options.note: Mark 8:34-35 gives the cross-shaped call of discipleship.

options.text: To give up some comforts and try to live a good life.
options.note: The call is far more radical: denying self and taking up the cross, not merely giving up comforts.

options.text: To surrender one's own agenda and follow Jesus wholeheartedly.
options.note: True in spirit, but it should include the striking image of taking up the cross and losing one's life for Him.

options.text: To gain as much of the world as possible for God's glory.
options.note: Jesus warns that gaining the world while forfeiting the soul profits nothing.

quiz.q: What warning does Jesus give about being ashamed of Him?

options.text: The Son of Man will be ashamed of that person when He comes in His Father's glory with the holy angels.
options.note: Mark 8:38; present shame of Christ leads to being disowned at His coming.

options.text: Such a person will simply miss out on some blessings.
options.note: The warning is far graver: being disowned by the Son of Man at His glorious coming.

options.text: The person will face Christ's disapproval when He returns in glory.
options.note: Close, but the text specifically says the Son of Man will be ashamed of, that is, disown, them.

options.text: Nothing will come of it, since God forgives everything anyway.
options.note: Jesus issues a real and solemn warning, not a promise that shame carries no consequence.

questions: Jesus asks each person directly, who do you say I am? How would you answer Him honestly today, and does your life match your words?
Peter confessed the Christ rightly yet recoiled from the cross. Where might you be embracing Jesus while resisting the suffering His way involves?
Jesus calls us to deny ourselves and take up the cross. What would self-denial concretely look like for you this week?
Jesus warns that gaining the whole world profits nothing if we forfeit our souls. Where are you tempted to trade what matters most for what matters least?
Jesus speaks of being ashamed of Him in an adulterous generation. In what settings are you tempted to hide your allegiance to Christ, and how might you own Him instead?

translationNotes.note: Must suffer translates a word of divine necessity; the cross is not an accident but the appointed way of the Christ, foretold in Scripture.
Life and soul render the one Greek word psyche; the saying turns on its double sense, physical life and the true self, so that saving the one can mean losing the other.

The kingdom of God arriving with power was seen within that generation in the transfiguration, the resurrection, and Pentecost, the decisive inbreaking of God's reign, not a prediction of the immediate end of the world.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Mark 8:27-38 and 9:1. Footnote markers and line breaks were dropped when assembling verse text. No em or en dashes appear in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
